lines: The old keep had stood empty for a hundred years, or so the villagers claimed, though nobody could say for certain why they still avoided the road that led to its gate. Ivy had swallowed the outer wall entirely, and the drawbridge chains had rusted into brittle orange lace. Still, every so often, a light flickered in the highest window, just after sunset, just long enough for someone walking past to notice and quicken their pace. The braver sort said it was nothing, just old glass catching the last of the sun. The wiser sort didn't say much of anything at all, and simply walked a little faster themselves.

Goblins, as a rule, are not known for their planning. They rush in loud, swing wildly, and are usually shocked when things go badly for them, which is often. This particular band, led by a goblin who had appointed himself Captain on the grounds that he owned the biggest hat, had decided that raiding the Slime King's territory was a fine idea. It was not. Within minutes, half the band had fled, the other half were thoroughly stuck, and the Captain had lost his hat somewhere in the retreat. He would, of course, tell the story differently later.

The market at dawn smelled of fresh bread, wet cobblestone, and the faint tang of dragon smoke drifting down from the mountain, a smell the townsfolk had long since stopped noticing. Merchants shouted over one another, hawking potions of questionable origin and blades that were, they swore, definitely enchanted. A young apprentice wizard wandered between the stalls, counting coins that clearly weren't enough for what she wanted, while a cat the size of a small dog watched the whole scene from a rooftop, entirely unbothered by any of it. Somewhere, a bell rang twice. Nobody paid it any attention at all.

There is a particular kind of silence that settles over a dungeon just before something goes wrong, and every adventurer worth their salt learns to recognize it eventually, usually the hard way. The torches burn a little too steady. The dust doesn't quite settle. Even the rats, normally content to scurry and squeak at will, go suspiciously quiet. Most people ignore this silence entirely, right up until the wall they were leaning on turns out to not be a wall at all, but something considerably less friendly. The lucky ones learn to listen. The rest become excellent cautionary tales, told around campfires for years to come.

The wizard's tower leaned at an angle that defied both architecture and common sense, a fact the wizard himself attributed to 'creative engineering' rather than the more likely explanation of a poorly cast spell some decades prior. Books were stacked in towers of their own throughout every room, arranged by a system only he understood, and possibly not even that. Visitors were rare, mostly because the front door required a riddle to open, and the wizard changed the riddle whenever he got bored, which was often. Those who did make it inside generally agreed it had been worth the trouble, assuming they survived the stairs.
